tutorial_welcome_body: A short tour of the workspace. You can leave any time and restart it from the compass icon in the top bar.
tutorial_surfaces_body: Move between Atmosphere (this globe), the Workbench (analysis), and Reflection (the methodology). These three anchors are always here on the left.
tutorial_dossier_body: A catalogue for examining the dataset itself — every probe, its sources, their metadata coverage and what AĒR can and cannot measure. It is for understanding what you are looking at, not for building an analysis.
tutorial_analyses_body: Store a Workbench view and reopen or share it later — your analyses live here.
tutorial_account_body: Profile, password, sessions and privacy controls. Language and appearance also live in here — and in the top bar.
tutorial_scopechip_body: This chip always shows your current surface and depth, so you never lose your place in the descent.
tutorial_utilities_body: Switch colour theme and interface language, step back and forward through your history, and sign out — all from the top-right of the bar.
tutorial_globe_body: Click a probe marker to select it; hold Shift and click to add more. A single click then offers “Open in Workbench”. Selected probes are your starting point.
tutorial_scopeeditor_body: Opening the Workbench (or “Open in Workbench” from a globe selection) brings up the Scope Editor. This is where you define your scope — which probes and sources you compare — before any analysis.
tutorial_scopegroups_body: Each card is a scope group: pick the probes and, within them, the sources you want to look at. Add more groups to compare side by side. This is exactly what your analysis will cover.
tutorial_scopeapply_title: Create the panel — off you go
tutorial_scopeapply_body: When your scope is set, “Create panel” builds your first panel and drops you into the Workbench. That is where the analysis begins.
tutorial_pillars_body: Aleph (the weather now), Episteme (the climate over time), and Rhizome (the currents between things). Switch stance here — the same data, three questions.
tutorial_panel_body: A panel holds your analysis of a scope. Its body shows one or more cells — each cell is a single visualisation. Use “⚙ Edit scope” on the panel to reopen the Scope Editor inline and change what it covers.
tutorial_panelcontrols_body: Set how a panel reads: composition (merge or split your scopes), presentation, metric, and the cell's fine configuration — all in this strip.
tutorial_panellabel_body: Give a panel a human caption (e.g. 'FR vs DE sentiment') so a saved analysis stays legible. Click the pencil to edit.
tutorial_cell_body: Each cell shows the data in one presentation, with its own export (PNG/SVG/CSV/JSON). When a panel splits across two or more sources, every cell also gets “Configure this cell” — override its settings independently, and it earns its own “How to read”. Here the panel is split across two sources so you can see it.
tutorial_addpanel_body: Add panels to compare scopes at a glance; each can be merged or split, and you choose how many share a row.
tutorial_readingguide_body: Every panel carries a plain-language guide — what it measures, the sample, the encoding, and its limits. Open it whenever a view is unfamiliar.
tutorial_zen_body: Send a cell, a panel, or the whole window full-screen to read it without distraction. Esc returns you.
tutorial_save_body: Store the current Workbench view to reopen or share later — it lives under Saved analyses on the left.
tutorial_reflectionnav_body: That covers the Workbench. The third surface — Reflection — is reachable any time from this rail anchor. Let's head there now.
tutorial_reflection_body: The methodology behind every number. When in doubt about what a metric means or how a probe was collected, this surface holds the reasoning. Here is what it gathers.
tutorial_reflectionquestions_body: The open research questions AĒR holds itself to — what is still unvalidated — and a primer on how to read the globe. Start here for the honest limits.
tutorial_reflectionpapers_body: The Working Papers (WP-001…WP-007) set out the scientific foundations: why each metric exists, how it is defined, and where it must not be over-read.
tutorial_reflectioncatalogues_body: Two reference catalogues: the probe dossiers (who is observed and how) and metric provenance (the exact lexicon, model or threshold behind every number).
tutorial_outro_body: You're ready to explore. You can re-open this guide any time from the compass icon in the top bar.
